preFix.title: How to trust your intuition
preFix.subtitle: What intuition actually is and how to distinguish it from fear
preFix.excerpt: Intuition is often described as a mystical sense. The more useful description is pattern recognition: the brain processing information faster than conscious thought can articulate. This reading explains what intuition is, what it isn't, and how to calibrate when to trust it.
preFix.sourceNotes: Synthesised from research on intuition, pattern recognition, and decision-making. No single source. Framing original to homemade.education.

content.text: Intuition is not a mystical sense. It is the brain processing accumulated experience: pattern recognition that operates faster than conscious reasoning. When you feel something is wrong before you can say why, the brain has matched the current situation to past patterns and reached a conclusion before the logical narrative has caught up.

Research on expert intuition, in surgeons, firefighters and chess players, finds that skilled practitioners learn to read situations fast and accurately in their domain. This isn't a special gift. It is accumulated exposure. The gut feeling is a summary of a lot of processed experience.

Intuition in your own life works the same way. When a friendship starts to feel wrong before you can say why, when a job offer feels uneasy despite checking all the boxes, when a place feels unsafe before any specific threat appears: these are the nervous system doing rapid matching against its history.
How to tell it from fear

The most useful distinction is between intuition and anxious projection. Fear projects outward: it generates scenarios about things that haven't happened and probably won't. Intuition is more often a quiet sense, still rather than spiralling, specific rather than general.
A useful question: is this a sense about something observable in the current situation, or a fear about what might be? Intuition responds to present data. Anxiety projects into the future.

Intuition is pattern recognition, which means it carries the biases of the patterns. If your history includes environments where certain people were threatening, your intuition may fire at people who resemble them, not because they are dangerous but because the pattern matches. A useful check: is this signal from what's here now, or from an old pattern I'm projecting onto a new situation?
Drawn from research on intuition and pattern recognition. No single source. Script by homemade.education.
